does: This paper introduces RoVRM, a visual reward model that improves alignment of large vision-language models with human preferences. The core contribution is a three-phase progressive training approach that leverages auxiliary textual preference data to overcome the scarcity of visual preference annotations. The method incorporates optimal transport-based preference data selection to effectively utilize both visual and textual preference signals. Experiments on LLaVA-1.5 models demonstrate that RoVRM consistently outperforms traditional visual reward models, reducing issues like hallucination where models generate misleading content without proper grounding in visual inputs.
connects: This work extends the line of research on human-preference alignment for vision-language models, building on prior approaches using best-of-n sampling and reinforcement learning for LVLM improvement. It responds to the key bottleneck of limited visual preference data availability, which has constrained the training of effective reward models. The paper connects to broader work on preference learning from human feedback and data scarcity mitigation in multimodal learning. By leveraging textual preferences as auxiliary supervision, it combines insights from cross-modal learning and preference-based reinforcement learning, addressing practical challenges in collecting sufficient visual annotations while benefiting from more readily available text-based preference data.
enables: RoVRM opens pathways for more practical deployment of preference-aligned vision-language models in real-world applications where visual hallucination poses risks. The demonstrated effectiveness of auxiliary textual data suggests future work could explore other modalities or data sources to bootstrap reward model training. The progressive training framework could inspire similar approaches for other multimodal tasks facing data scarcity. Additionally, this work enables development of better reinforcement learning pipelines for LVLMs without requiring large-scale human visual annotation efforts, potentially accelerating research on honest and contextually-grounded visual reasoning systems.
